The psychological impact of home-stadium cheering significantly alters the performance metrics of competing teams. In one study of American football fan behaviors in the second half of play [3], home-stating fans were significantly less likely to cheer until injuries occurred and higher in their team's probability of winning compared to people living in venues away from fans of their sport and in situations where fans often did nothing—at no point were home team's probability of loss above 50% and home-state advantage at 70% when cheerleading was taking place on the sidelines during a break. One-half of both cheerleading teams and all sports fan groups cheered the last 30 minutes of play after they knew there would at least be 30 minutes of additional action from game break to regulation—no significant changes were seen among those not participating in the studies that focused only upon fans living in an away venue or in cases lacking home team rivalry in the arena they attended game. Similarly, it is known for fans in sports that they often experience elevated levels of arousal in anticipation and then during event-related cognitive function [8–10]. In a review of sports entertainment in terms of performance psychology, one of its central ideas emphasized that these differences cannot be attributed solely to a unique physiological experience that could impact attention, thinking, and emotion more broadly than other physiological factors.

There is an abundance of published data, from all coaches and for all coaches across the world. It highlights clearly that a football philosophy is developed that ultimately makes teams who can defeat opponents – even "win by the smallest chance" – and this principle can be transferred to a defensive game style. This provides for an important change to professional football: an idea from tactical and technical analysis, an intuition from the experience, and, most often, a willingness to give everything in order to succeed. Power profiling allows us to determine specific physiological parameters that, in aggregate, account for "peak performances", to ensure that a full 1-minute power curve is formed, resulting in 3:10, with the athlete being within a small enough margin to achieve his or her competitive performance potential, thereby, maximizing the ability for endurance athletes.

This new and revolutionary power tool helps determine that 1-minute power curve, without relying on power output tests and a few minutes of "power-building workouts," so athletes of virtually any age/athletic level will not have access to "training variables" that can affect long term fitness.

Okay, so there I was, staring face-to-face with a literal dragon, holding nothing but a stupid plastic fork. No, no. This was just a hallucination triggered from a bad dream, I assured her. She took a step towards me then suddenly jumped back away... just shy. She said she saw an explosion, didn't say anything, then ran outside. Her friend who was hiding in the barn was still hiding. She took some time to come down before telling me she had been playing in the yard during the day when the dragons began harassing and attacking. So when I got up to follow her I couldn't tell. My head hurt. What exactly had gone wrong in her life today so bad you would do anything possible not to live it as far away as possible? That seemed to be the prevailing question coming through in her voice. And yet something odd remained: even though we never talked for the rest of those three-quarters of a century (well except by mail) I just kept believing there was a chance that it wasn't happening again. And so I went and joined my friend, and with her encouragement and all the help from her family and my school friends (oh, how sweet) we set off at 11 a.m. to try and run them down.

Imagine waking up one day and discovering you can completely pause time just by snapping your fingers. That is the premise for this week on The Sporkful.

What time is it?

You want to understand how time changes for us as much as we want to understand how we time change now. The last thing you should do is be trying to figure out your age by looking in an oven or a clock. What the fuck am I doing?

Time is more mysterious than clocks, more mysterious than ovens

Time moves around and moves backwards. Your watch tells the time but not as much time as it also tells where we were at one point in time with no precision.

At any particular time a given amount of time has passed since a moment. At first, our brains make no sense of the difference; it doesn't exist and can only operate from a limited set of past time frames. In fact, that amount of time, a moment now, represents 0 minutes and 1 hour and 45 minutes of time ago for us. We feel these minutes and hours have been ticking away forever and ever forever.

For people born in 1817 we seem to perceive these 0 and 00 seconds as two periods of time as if all time was one gigantic "long instant."

Time now: you

So this mad scientist guy down the block built a time machine out of an old rusty microwave, right? What do I have it be so big that the world won't touch me with a giant, shiny metal object, anyway?" I asked. "Oh I just have it in it for good reason." A few seconds of thought about everything my professor told me led us down the path. First, I wanted to give time machines just that little chance to do some good; my idea was nothing more than the result of careful consideration and reasoning. A large building where I could take on monsters or other villains from my time. Not to mention the bonus that this room might hold some sort of weapon!

"But Professor!" I was only on half thought.

"My friend, just so that you guys know, I had an interesting thought about it. In order to get it in my laboratory a way bigger, there has to be a way," he said and went behind a shelf containing three boxes. I guess that the last two might actually be weapons (I wasn't paying attention) and so he chose something in the corner.

"What a coincidence. A Time Weapon just so happens on you here. Now what are you going to do with it?", was the last thing I heard before he walked back into the lab.

A comparative study of modern crossovers reveals distinct advantages in terms of fuel efficiency and safety ratings. This paper describes recent innovations from Mercedes Benz which enhance this capability of modern small block V8, particularly involving emissions systems and power reduction technologies. New emissions control technologies implemented at Mercedes Benz from 2014 to present, are able now to reduce by roughly 40-42 percent the emissions over the old-generation direct injectors in V8 (see below Figure 7). Since the engines of today have considerably less torque than the diesel motors we can already see considerable savings from the combination of power reduction with efficiency. These benefits have allowed us significantly surpass Volkswagen Group with a higher combined production of over 300 thousand V8's since September 2014. These engines, together with direct injection system, have resulted into reduced maintenance costs on an average of 1 year and 1 week according to Euro-Green engine and gearbox supplier data.

The mitigation of industrial carbon dioxide emissions remains a primary objective for international environmental policy. However, while carbon removal systems are well known, and are commercially viable in some environments, the benefits that they offer have recently become less clear, while also not being sufficiently understood. In this Review we focus on studies that demonstrate how different technologies with associated economics and technical challenges can potentially contribute to industrial carbon capture. Although many other areas of applied science (chemistry, mineral analysis, metallurgy, etc.) as well as in industrial engineering are being explored to better understanding of industrial carbon emissions, this review addresses the current challenges faced and opportunities of such applications on a broad scale, such as: industrial processes (oil-based, biogas-free etc.), and of CO 2 extraction via CO treatment and/or water extraction (liquid hydrocarbon etc.), carbon extraction from geological formations. Thus in this review we highlight potential opportunities by comparing potential mitigation, sequestration and capture solutions, using various technologies in more context. This could guide industrial policy decision makers and guide industry design of new facilities or improvements to existing technology, as well as helping the development of improved equipment etc.

Policy analysts evaluate the efficacy of carbon taxing as a mechanism to curb industrial pollutant output. These assessments typically determine that under existing policies the tax does generate revenue—usually tens of billions a year. Most agree this revenue provides a sufficient safety valve for emissions as other options are inadequate to fully mitigate atmospheric acidification.

Despite a consensus, an industry-focused advocacy group—CERL, or "Climate Education and Solutions—takes up any opposition to the use (or misuses) of the taxes. The effort seeks to educate the public, policymakers, business community and policy makers about how CERS may not work or have serious unintended consequences. Its latest push, launched late last year with a report entitled, "Climate Action is Cost Limited" calls carbon pricing the most effective long-term mechanism for addressing climate change. CERL advocates the use of taxes to limit greenhouse emissions and calls it a superior option than using regulation to deal with climate change. But critics charge the group's stance lacks any nuance by emphasizing a tax can be as economically valuable as regulation while providing no guarantees any reduction in carbon.

The study of global climate patterns reveals accelerated melting trajectories in polar ice sheets. From Antarctica at the highest elevations, melt could lead to rising Sea Level by approximately 1 m. By late this century, sea level rise with accelerated land ice melts should be seen worldwide, but Antarctica is projected to serve the large majority (> 80 %) of total warming induced polar sea-level contributions.

The largest contributors are polar ice-sheets of West Antarctica (3–5 m), East Antarctica (8–16 m), Greenland (~20 cm), both Arctic (~40 mm) and mid-latitude (~13 mm) regions. Submarine canisters (for methane hydrates) in submarine ice are not included in sea-level scenarios but could accelerate warming trends at the margin. However, submarine gas hydrates are considered a less likely contributor globally due to their higher potential greenhouse gas feedback value since their rates of decay are faster than water temperature (6).

An objective assessment of daily caffeine intake reveals its distinct physiological impact on sleep architecture. Our data shows clear-cut differences in the quality of alertness upon the change from low (>200 mg/day caffeine content) to moderate (0–40 mg/day), and higher (>40 mg/day) daily and cumulative doses. Specifically, sleep architecture is dramatically altered with acute high (∼10 mg/day) doses, indicating alterations to sleep propensity or sleep control that may underlie a multitude of health risks commonly seen after caffeine use in people with established diseases or impaired circadian biology; whereas, we also observed that low (∼12 mg/day) as opposed to high (40 mg/day) doses resulted in no change to sleep architecture. Further details of caffeine's effects following acute, chronic (8 days; daily at rest or following strenuous exercise) and cumulative (≥10 years) consumption will need to be further evaluated. Our data supports the importance of the individual's dose profile in relation to total daily intake to effectively optimise cognitive function and sleep. The precise tracking of daily caloric expenditure is a foundational component of evidence-based weight management. This review examined the validity of food intake profiles derived from the direct methods that are currently used, as well as other non-invasive and indirect information, including weight data drawn from daily questionnaires, self-reported, doubly labeled water, metabolic panel examinations, electrooculography, and radioiodine emissions in rats and in dogs. In rats, the accuracy of using 3-D food intake profiles and validated questionnaires as a guide for determining daily energy intake is extremely poor due to a significant difference at the protein and micronutrient levels between the measured and reference food intake levels for 3 standard meals. There was little agreement among methods assessing energy expenditure, and even the 2 most precise methods of diet reporting have substantial internal measurement error rates and lack of correspondence between calculated intake and measured ad libitum, body weight. In dogs, no validated method is available to accurately estimate body weights from indirect estimates of 24-hr energy expenditure or from food restriction and refeeding. However, body composition can accurately be estimated from 1 measure, urinary osmolality, with a margin of error greater than 5,000/L, whereas body density can be estimated from either urinary urea nitrogen or plasma nitrogen profile data. The hypothalamo, brain, peripheral blood, and vascular functions and cardiovascular adaptation to the weight-reducing challenge of a low calorie diet depend on caloric and macronutrient composition. We show that diet-induced weight gain, as defined by a reduction of 2.5% below baseline weight and body weight of lean (body mass index less than 30 kg/m 2 ) humans or rodent models, can be minimized and significantly reversed following the addition of adequate caloric supplementation to normal diets without alterations in essential brain or cellular function. These effects occur without changes in protein-calcium (Ca²) homeostasis . We propose that energy restriction, through altering the macronutrient composition, causes sustained negative energy balance with decreased Ca² fluxes but with changes in extracellular matrix (ECM) composition and that these structural alterations contribute to enhanced adaptive thermogenesis. Moreover, when animals are sleep deprived for sufficient duration the consolidation mechanisms are robust across various training/test situations (Shapiro and Stuckler, 1991; Schapiro, 2005; Schapiro and Shapiro, 2008) suggesting the mechanism has long-term adaptive capacities rather than mere maintenance or short-term extinction. Entertainment industry analysts observe a distinct shift in consumer preferences toward serialized television dramas. "The average audience is becoming older," says Andrew Dabb, head of strategy and business development at TV networks including HBO and Showtime, which own The Hour. "With that comes the idea that you want the season to tell you where you're going to go next from time to time with episodic programming." To meet the demand is to invest heavily in expensive, high-quality programming with huge lead-frames, or three-episode-a-week blocks of content that serve a single major storyline. This is increasingly the dominant business model for television, which in a world of declining ad revenues often delivers greater profits for individual showrunners who keep more story, like HBO's Westworld or its forthcoming hit, The Girlfriend Experience. It's also true in many big-budget prestige dramas such as the recent Twin Peaks, where each and every episode is an entire season in and of itself.

TV ratings have plummeted significantly due to consumers becoming disenchanted with linear TV (that is, the traditional broadcast signal, consisting essentially of programs that have had three episodes' worth placed in sequence on local or U.S. networks). The structural integrity of a film's third act dictates its overall thematic and narrative success among critics.

To the best of my knowledge, a third act of a film is always a necessary but not sufficient catalyst for its overall success. The second acts are often a result of one or more characters realizing what they must do in order to prevail over their antagonist. For films which center upon individual heroes, the third act is not the main motivating force. In films dealing with larger themes and characters, the third act is as crucial as that in any other genre, and can be just as pivotal in bringing about dramatic change in an otherwise well grounded third act. In any given film, the success or failure of the third act determines how many points of contention can be raised after the final credits roll, or to the movie itself. (See "In Closing"),


On the flipside, a film that uses its third act to give away many plot secrets about how the central character gets on is the exception to the rule, and the second or third act of a three-act play often has a very different tonal, dramatic and thematic outlook.

File checkpointing uses file-level snapshots of the local file system or an extended file system by employing an incremental restore feature through automatic back-ups of directory tree entries and snapshots for a specific file name entry in the tree of files. All checkpoint checkpoints were constructed with full file verification (a file is present is either a current, signed snapshot or is a fully compressed fully signed copy in the file system root filesystem directory, or if an incremental checkpoint, at or at least prior to its current version), and, where applicable, user-mode encryption. The architectural design of open-world environments significantly impacts the player's cognitive immersion and pathfinding. Since navigation in MMOFPS games is intrinsically constrained – for players to leave areas of their play-world or return home often requires traveling on a set path along an unnavigable surface – this design challenge also dictates other design elements as well. For instance, in Dark Souls, certain buildings and other static elements on which players explore may exist along the ground – with players frequently stumbling across doors and stairs before knowing of the existence below. Thus, open-ended design in games allows the construction of physical objects – by providing the environment designers a consistent method for manipulating these objects – while keeping their meaning, function and the experience of traversing the place clear to the player.

In an open-world role-playing game design, a set of building constraints, set to interact to a reasonable degree over an individual build-and-repair process, would make room for interaction with the environment as well as player agency. A typical build would generally occur throughout an area rather than in a static location. As in all aspects involved in building a game world such as character interaction or building a level layout, it's imperative that design provide space for the player in both gameplay and environmental spaces. Public defenders emphasize the absolute necessity of preserving constitutional protections during criminal trials. While it is legal to use this interrogation practice against the uncooperative criminal defendant, this practice would normally not be used to obtain a confession unless the interrogator feared that a confession would be a vital piece of evidence — for example, as part of a plea agreement for trial without a jury when there is no possibility of a judge rendering a confession. "It is a serious abuse, [as it] forces vulnerable people who have done nothing illegal to accept, sometimes, the kind of harsh tactics in search of confessions with which this system is known." Many of the cases investigated at length in the new ACLU report focus not primarily on a particular legal rule of evidence, but on judicial practices concerning confession suppression that place greater emphasis on interrogation technique—even though confession evidence was virtually nonexistent in most cases during this three-year interval reviewed. Criminologists evaluate the correlation between community policing initiatives and public trust in urban areas. They conduct various surveys, studies, surveys, and other inquiries and conclude when and why communities are underprivileged and vulnerable. In most urban contexts where the poor living around the police become part of a police-community, this relationship results in the creation of lawless and chaotic neighborhoods, police abuse, and violence against people targeted by police. One consequence is more police harassment, which eventually creates an atmosphere similar to those surrounding prison cells (Alkharaf 1997), thus undermining community trust (Jardina 1998; Eisner 2000). By creating unsafe streets and living in a hostile environment, we are also increasing the probability of police involvement in violence-related offenses, thereby further entrenching a system of social order. It is important to remember the link between crime and institutionalization. Although people are imprisoned for some criminal offenses, that fact hardly affects our understanding if police are primarily or only involved with those individuals. But that point should be understood in its full extent.

The economic consequences of trade tariff implementation remain a central point of debate among prominent analysts. While they tend to support free trade among members of their country's market in terms of benefits to each country, they are more skeptical that such free riding will ever occur. While members of a national market share a degree of latitude on tariff imposition, their individual policies, particularly tariff ceilings, remain tied to overall market shares and competitiveness within that country.[4]

Some countries are trying to prevent tariff hikes using tariffs themselves on international merchandise, arguing that lowering protection causes price distortions. Another common strategy is creating barriers on foreign trade so that goods exported to countries with the high cost of foreign goods cannot escape import tariffs if imported domestically.[5] For more details about such economic-cost-reduction policies see Appendix: Cost and Competition Studies of Antitrust Strategies. In both cases, tariffs are being levied in order to reduce overall consumer price levels of trade, and those tariffs should be allowed to stand until any countervailing effect from trade with the subsidized country should clear.[6 the text]. Other countries, who were members of the NAFTA cartel without an agreed price ceiling, tend to believe that their membership in that club makes them bound to uphold fair-trade principles as part of their national sovereignty.
